stem: A manufacturing study measures defect rate across 5 factorys, each containing multiple production lines. factory A has 10 observations with mean 69.1 % (within-factory SD = 5.2). factory B has 15 observations with mean 40.8 % (within-factory SD = 4.2). factory C has 15 observations with mean 65.2 % (within-factory SD = 9.8). factory D has 5 observations with mean 69.8 % (within-factory SD = 7.5). factory E has 15 observations with mean 65.7 % (within-factory SD = 8.6). The between-factory standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 7.7 and 19.4 %. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true defect rate for factory B, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-factory variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for factory B: 40.9 % (shrunk from raw 40.8). Range [40.9, 41.2] as between-factory SD varies from 7.7 to 19.4. Pooled mean: 60.3.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 60.3 % (weighted average of 5 factory means).
Shrinkage factor for factory B: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/15). At σ_between=12.9: B = 0.993.
Interval [40.9, 41.2] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (40.8) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (60.3) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
